prerequisites.question: In physics, a force is best described as:
options: a push or a pull that can change an object's motion
the speed at which an object is moving
the amount of matter an object contains
the energy stored inside a moving object
prerequisites.answer: a push or a pull that can change an object's motion
prerequisites.remediation: A force is a push or pull. A NET force changes an object's velocity (Newton's second law, F = ma); with no net force, motion just continues unchanged. Today, gravity is the force doing all the pushing and pulling.

prerequisites.question: You whirl a ball on a string in a circle at steady speed. To keep it circling, your hand must pull the string:
options: inward, toward the center of the circle
outward, away from the center
forward, in the direction the ball is moving
no pull is needed once the ball is moving
prerequisites.answer: inward, toward the center of the circle
prerequisites.remediation: Circular motion needs a constant inward (centripetal) pull. Cut the string and the ball flies off in a straight line. In an orbit, gravity is that inward pull — always aimed at the central body.

prerequisites.question: A data table shows that when x doubles, y grows to MORE than double. This tells you y rises:
options: faster than in direct proportion to x
in exact direct proportion to x
more slowly than x
not at all as x changes
prerequisites.answer: faster than in direct proportion to x
prerequisites.remediation: If y more than doubles when x doubles, y climbs faster than proportionally — a power law y proportional to x^p with p > 1. Reading a table for exactly this kind of pattern is how Kepler cracked his Third Law.

beats.title: Why doesn't the Moon fall down?
beats.goal: Hook with the apple-and-Moon insight. A thrown ball arcs back to Earth — everything falls. Then confront the student: the Moon feels that same gravity, so why doesn't it crash down? Pose the mystery, no laws yet.

skeleton.title: Why doesn't the Moon fall down?

skeleton.text: Toss a ball into the air and it arcs straight back to the ground. On Earth, everything falls.
Gravity won; the ball landed. Now look up at the Moon — the very same gravity reaches all the way out and pulls on it too.
So here is Newton's wild question: if gravity tugs the Moon exactly as it tugged your ball, why doesn't the Moon come crashing down on us? Hold that thought.

beats.title: Falling sideways: the first orbit
beats.goal: Newton's cannonball. Fire a projectile flatter and faster; the faster it goes the farther it falls before landing — until, at orbital speed, the ground curves away as fast as it drops and it never lands. An orbit is perpetual falling.

skeleton.text: Newton pictured a cannon on an impossibly tall mountain, firing flat. Fire harder, and the ball lands farther away.
Faster still, and the Earth curves away beneath the ball as fast as it falls — so it keeps missing the ground. That is an orbit: perpetual falling.

For Earth that magic speed is about {{round(sqrt(3.986e14/6.371e6)/1e2)/10}} km every second. Predict before you slide: fire even faster than that — does the ball fall back, circle forever, or fly off and escape?

check.text: Newton imagined firing a cannonball ever faster from a mountaintop. At orbital speed, the cannonball:
options: keeps falling toward Earth but moves sideways so fast it never lands
stops feeling gravity once it is fast enough
travels in a perfectly straight line forever
needs a rocket engine burning constantly to stay up
check.answer: keeps falling toward Earth but moves sideways so fast it never lands

beats.title: Circles first: which way does the force point?
beats.goal: Start with the simplest orbit — a perfect circle. The velocity points along the path (tangent), but the force that bends it must point inward, to the center. Confront the direction-of-force misconception head on with a free-body diagram beside the circle.

skeleton.title: Circles first: which way does the force point?

skeleton.text: Begin with the simplest orbit — a perfect circle. The green velocity arrow always points along the path, sideways, never at the Sun.
Predict before you look: the single force acting on this planet — which way does it point? Forward along its motion, or somewhere else?
Here is your answer: gravity points neither forward nor back, but straight inward — toward the Sun at the center.
That inward pull never speeds the planet up or slows it down; it only bends its straight-line path into a circle. That is what 'centripetal' means: center-seeking.

check.text: For a planet in a circular orbit, the net force on it points:
options: toward the Sun, at the center of the circle
forward, in the direction the planet is moving
outward, away from the Sun
nowhere — there is no net force if the speed is constant
check.answer: toward the Sun, at the center of the circle

beats.title: The data rebels: a war on Mars
beats.goal: Tension beat. For 2000 years orbits HAD to be circles. Kepler began there too, armed with Tycho Brahe's superb data — but Mars refused to fit any circle, missing by a stubborn 8 arcminutes. Kepler trusted the data over the beautiful circle. Light on visuals, heavy on drama.

skeleton.title: The data rebels: a war on Mars

skeleton.text: For two thousand years, orbits HAD to be perfect circles — the ideal shape of the heavens. Kepler started there too, armed with twenty years of Tycho Brahe's superb naked-eye measurements.
Now make the call before he did: when his best circle clashed with Tycho's Mars, which should Kepler have trusted — the beautiful theory, or the stubborn data?
He trusted the data. His best circle missed Tycho's Mars by a stubborn 8 arcminutes — a quarter the width of the full Moon.
So he threw out the sacred circle and asked a heretical question: then what shape IS it?

check.text: Why did Kepler finally abandon circular orbits?
options: Tycho's precise data disagreed with every circle by a small but stubborn 8 arcminutes
circles were mathematically too difficult to compute
the telescope had not been invented, so he was only guessing
he preferred ellipses for religious reasons
check.answer: Tycho's precise data disagreed with every circle by a small but stubborn 8 arcminutes

beats.title: First Law: the shape of an orbit
beats.goal: Kepler's answer: the ellipse, with the Sun at one focus — never the center. Eccentricity sets the stretch. Let the student predict where the Sun's focus goes as e grows, then test with the slider.

skeleton.text: The shape that fit was an ellipse — a stretched circle — with the Sun sitting at one focus, off to one side, never at the center.
Eccentricity e measures the stretch: e = 0 is a perfect circle, and the larger e grows, the more elongated the orbit becomes.
Predict before you slide: as you push e toward 0.9, does the Sun's focus drift toward the center, or out toward the edge?

check.text: In an elliptical orbit, where is the Sun located?
options: at one focus, off to one side
at the exact geometric center of the ellipse
out at the far edge of the ellipse
at both foci at the same time
check.answer: at one focus, off to one side

beats.title: Second Law: equal areas in equal times
beats.goal: Resolve the hook's speed puzzle. The Sun-planet line sweeps equal areas in equal times, so the planet MUST race when close and crawl when far. Show the gold wedge gliding round the orbit, then let the student scrub it and predict where speed peaks.

skeleton.title: Second Law: equal areas, unequal speeds

skeleton.text: The gold wedge is the area the Sun-planet line sweeps out in one fixed slice of time. Watch it travel all the way around.
Near the Sun the wedge is short and fat; far out it is long and thin — yet the area is always identical. Equal areas, equal times.
Scrub t yourself and compare the wedge at the near point with the far point. Where must the planet race fastest to keep each wedge equal?

check.text: Kepler's Second Law (equal areas in equal times) means a planet moves:
options: fastest when closest to the Sun
fastest when farthest from the Sun
at exactly the same speed all the way around
fastest exactly halfway between closest and farthest
check.answer: fastest when closest to the Sun

beats.title: Third Law: the harmony of the worlds
beats.goal: Kepler's Third Law from the real solar system. Show measured a and T for Mercury through Mars, spot that bigger orbits take disproportionately longer years, then derive T = a^(3/2) in solar units and check it against Mars's measured year with the verified kepler3 fact.

skeleton.title: Third Law: T squared tracks a cubed

skeleton.text: Here is the real data Kepler pored over for years — each planet's orbit size a and its year T. Notice: as a grows, T grows even faster.

skeleton.title: Spot the pattern: T squared vs a cubed
notes: period squared is proportional to axis cubed
for the same Sun, take the year in Earth-units
raise Mars's a = 1.524 AU to the three-halves power
the law nails Tycho's measured Martian year

skeleton.text: Test it on Mars: raising a = 1.524 AU to the three-halves power predicts a year of {{kepler3(1,1,1.524)}} Earth-years.
And the sky agrees — Mars really takes 1.88 years. Every planet obeys it: T squared tracks a cubed. Now predict a comet at a = 9 AU: how long is its year?

check.text: A comet orbits the same Sun with semi-major axis a = 9 AU. Using T squared proportional to a cubed, its orbital period is:
options: {{kepler3(1,1,9)}} years
9 years
18 years
81 years

beats.title: Newton: the one force behind it all
beats.goal: Reveal the engine. Newton's inverse-square gravity IS the centripetal force an orbit needs. Set them equal, substitute the orbital speed, and out drops T squared proportional to r cubed — with the planet's own mass cancelling. Newton doesn't fit Kepler; he explains him.

skeleton.title: Newton: gravity is the centripetal force

skeleton.text: Here is Newton's law: every mass pulls every other with a force that fades as 1 over r squared — and for a planet, that inward pull IS the centripetal force you need.

skeleton.title: Gravity gives you Kepler's Third Law
notes: gravity supplies the centripetal force m v squared over r
orbital speed is one circumference per period
substitute v, and the planet's mass m cancels off both sides
T squared is proportional to r cubed — Kepler's Third Law, constant revealed

skeleton.text: Before we substitute the orbital speed, make the call: when you set gravity equal to the centripetal force, does the planet's own mass m cancel out — or survive into the answer?

Watch it happen: m sits on both sides and cancels, which is exactly why every planet obeys the same law whatever its size. You are left with T squared = (4 pi squared / GM) r cubed, its constant pinned to the Sun's mass.

check.text: In this derivation, why does the planet's own mass m disappear from the final result?
options: it appears on both the gravity side and the centripetal side, so it cancels
we assumed the planet is massless
Newton measured it to be exactly zero
it was never written into the equations
check.answer: it appears on both the gravity side and the centripetal side, so it cancels

beats.title: Weighing the Sun without leaving home
beats.goal: The power beat. Run Newton's law backwards: from Earth's orbit radius and year alone, solve for the Sun's mass. Every input is measurable from Earth, the planet's mass cancels, and the board computes M_sun to the accepted value. Pure leverage.

skeleton.title: Weighing the Sun without leaving home
The Sun's mass from Earth's orbit alone
notes: rearrange Newton's law to solve for the central mass M
plug in Earth's orbit radius and its year in seconds
every quantity here is measured from Earth — none from the Sun
the Sun weighs about two thousand trillion trillion tonnes

skeleton.text: Now flip the very same equation around: from Earth's orbit radius r and its year T alone, you can solve for the Sun's mass M.
Every number you plug in — r, T, and G — you measured from right here on Earth. Before the board evaluates it, take a guess: will the Sun's mass land bigger or smaller than 10 to the 30 kilograms?
You just weighed the Sun without leaving home: {{4*pi^2*(1.496e11)^3/(6.674e-11*(3.156e7)^2*1e30)}} times ten-to-the-thirty kilograms — and the accepted value is 1.989. Nailed it.

check.text: We computed the Sun's mass from Earth's orbit alone. What made that possible?
options: the planet's mass cancels, so the period and radius alone fix the central mass M
we first had to weigh the Earth
the Sun's mass was already hidden inside the constant G
we needed to measure the Sun's temperature and size
check.answer: the planet's mass cancels, so the period and radius alone fix the central mass M

beats.title: Parking a satellite: geostationary orbit
beats.goal: Apply the machinery to modern life. A geostationary satellite must circle once per Earth spin; that single period, fed through Kepler's Third Law, fixes its altitude uniquely. Compute the real radius and height every TV satellite sits at.

skeleton.text: A geostationary satellite has to hang fixed over one spot on the equator — so you must give it an orbital period that exactly matches Earth's spin.

skeleton.title: How high must it orbit?
notes: match Earth's true spin — one sidereal day
Kepler's Third Law, rearranged to solve for the radius
that one period fixes the radius uniquely
subtract Earth's radius to get the height above the ground

skeleton.text: Predict before the math runs: to keep pace with Earth's spin, will the satellite have to sit higher or lower than the Moon's orbit?

Fix the period to one day and Kepler's Third Law nails the radius at {{round((3.986e14*86164^2/(4*pi^2))^(1/3)/1e3)}} km — far below the Moon. So every TV and weather satellite you use sits {{round(((3.986e14*86164^2/(4*pi^2))^(1/3)-6.378e6)/1e3)}} km overhead, one altitude forced by one law.

check.text: To stay fixed above one spot on the equator, a satellite's orbital period must equal one Earth day. What does that requirement do to its altitude?
options: it fixes the altitude uniquely, at about {{round(((3.986e14*86164^2/(4*pi^2))^(1/3)-6.378e6)/1e6)*1e3}} km
the altitude is free — you can park it at any height
it must orbit as low as possible, just above the atmosphere
the altitude depends on the satellite's mass
check.answer: it fixes the altitude uniquely, at about {{round(((3.986e14*86164^2/(4*pi^2))^(1/3)-6.378e6)/1e6)*1e3}} km

beats.title: One law, the whole sky
beats.goal: Synthesis. Put all three laws in one moving picture and name the single cause beneath them — Newton's inverse-square gravity. Tease the transfer orbit that carries spacecraft between planets, and test the big-picture unification.

skeleton.text: One picture, three laws: an ellipse with the Sun at a focus (First), a gold equal-area wedge sweeping round (Second), sized so T squared tracks a cubed (Third).
And beneath all three lies one single cause — Newton's 1 over r squared gravity, forever aimed at the Sun.

rows: orbit is an ellipse, Sun at one focus
equal areas in equal times — fastest when nearest
T squared is proportional to a cubed
gravity F = GMm/r squared causes all of it

skeleton.text: Same law everywhere: it holds moons and satellites, and — with a gentle nudge between two ellipses — carves the transfer orbit that flings a spacecraft from Earth to Mars.
Replay any orbit you like with the slider — from a near-circle to a wild comet, every shape still obeys all four rules at once.

check.text: Which single physical law, applied to orbits, produces ALL THREE of Kepler's laws?
options: Newton's law of universal gravitation (the inverse-square law)
conservation of mass
the ideal gas law
Hooke's law for springs
check.answer: Newton's law of universal gravitation (the inverse-square law)
